dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 for a reported unknown problem/person down at 123 Maple Street.
Hi, my name is Sarah, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing for several days now, and it's getting worse.
Mr. Thomas, can you tell me your name?
Thomas, T-H-O-M-A-S.
That's great. Are you aware of where you are?
Yes, I think I am in Richmond.
Thank you. Can you tell me why the 911 number was called?
I've been feeling short of breath, like I can't get enough air in.
Have other symptoms been present, like chest pain or fever?
No chest pain, but I do have a persistent cough.
Mr. Thomas, your eyes are open and alert, which is good to see.
Your verbal responses are clear and coherent. That's important for our full exam.
When I asked you to respond, you did so with full awareness and cooperation.
Mr. Thomas, are you usually on oxygen at home?
Yes, two liters via nasal cannula for my COPD.
Mr. Thomas, on 2 liters of oxygen, your saturation is at 99%. That's good.
Your breathing seems rapid. May I assess your breath sounds with the stethoscope?
Your respiratory rate is 24 and appears labored, but no wheezing on exam yet.
You have rhonchi bilaterally and some wheezing noted.
Pulse is 90 and regular, strong.
Blood pressure is 176 over 69-slightly elevated but stable.
Mr. Thomas, your skin is warm, dry, and without cyanosis.
Have you experienced cough or chest tightness with your breathing difficulty?
Yes, I have a productive cough but can't bring up the mucus.
What chronic conditions do you have on your medical report?
COPD, non-insulin-dependent diabetes, and mild kidney disease, Stage 2.
What medications are you taking now?
Aloprim, Cymbalta, Flonase, Lasix, and Levaquin.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Has this shortness of breath started recently or is it worse than earlier?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: It's been going on for several days and just got worse.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Any recent visits to the hospital or ER?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: A couple of days ago for cellulitis, but it wasn't with shortness of breath then.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Does the difficulty getting air happen more at rest or when active?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: More when I try to move around. I sit most of the time now.
We found bilateral expiratory wheezing and continue to monitor your breathing.
The patient stated he is currently on oxygen through a nasal cannula at home, which he is continuing today.
We found rhonchi lessening as we evaluated-some wheezing still during exhalation.
I noted you have a tape allergy, so I will avoid using any adhesive skin tape.
We found your skin well-maintained for age and no signs of jaundice or rashes.
Let's check your blood sugar just to confirm no unexpected spikes.
Blood sugar is 134. No hyperglycemia observed at this time.
Pulse is 90 with no irregularities at this point.
Your respiratory rate is steady at 24 per minute-showing signs of exertion breathing.
Blood pressure remains at 176 over 69.
On 2 liters, your oxygen saturation is 99%. No cyanosis seen.
We are placing you on the cardiac monitor to examine any irregular rhythms.
The EKG suggests Atrial Fibrillation. Let's get a more detailed reading.
A 12-lead EKG is about to be performed per standard protocol.
The EKG results confirm Atrial Fibrillation with premature ventricular contractions, a left axis deviation, and nonspecific intraventricular conduction block.
Mr. Thomas, we are giving you a nebulizer with two medications: Atrovent and Albuterol.
That's fine. Please go ahead with the treatment.
I will place an IV in your left arm for potential future medications.
Saline lock is established and ready for use if needed.
Have you been having any pain with your breathing or while lying down?
No, my breathing is difficult, but not painful.
You have report of no current pain, so no analgesics will be given at this time.
Rechecking pulse shows a drop to 80, which is now in a normal range.
Respiratory rate has slightly decreased. Now at 20 per minute.
Your blood pressure is 159 over 81- stable and lower now.
Blood sugar became inaccessible during transport.
Oxygen saturation remains at 98-99% during movement.
The EKG still shows atrial fibrillation with occasional PVCs.
Based on your symptoms and our findings, we are treating this under respiratory distress protocol.
Protocol also notes underlying Atrial Fibrillation and chest movements indicating congestive heart failure.
Mr. Thomas, where would you like to go for care today?
I prefer to go to St. Francis Medical Center.
Please try to stand slowly while I assist you to the stretcher.
Mr. Thomas, I'm here with you. Let's go at your pace.
You're doing really well. I'm right here with you.
You're safely secured and set for transport. Please keep your limbs inside during the ride.
All straps are double checked and tightened.
We are now en route to St. Francis Medical Center.
You're doing great, and we'll keep monitoring you the whole way to the hospital.
We completed all transport checks, and no changes to your condition so far.
Upon arrival, a skin tear on the left forearm was noted during movement of the oxygen tank.
No bleeding or pain was reported to the site, and the ER has been informed.
Patient is stable per ongoing assessment and continuously monitored in transport per medical protocol.
His vitals remain stable. He tolerated all initial interventions well.
The nebulization treatment has resulted in no symptomatic improvement per visual assessment.
A 12-lead EKG is available for review at the hospital. Results are consistent with underlying conditions.
Mr. Thomas was transported without complications and remains pain-free.
No additional findings on reassessment during transport. No alterations in care needed.
We have reached the hospital. Time for safe transfer to the ER stretcher, Mr. Thomas.
Will move you using the draw sheet method to avoid further skin issues.
No sign of discomfort noted during the transfer. All ready for handoff.
We're now working to transfer you to the ER stretcher as planned.
Patient is ready for transfer to the emergency room in room 7.
Full EPCR is transmitted for ER documentation and ongoing care.
The patient is now fully handed over to ER care and ready for treatment.
Mr. Thomas received a DuoNeb with Atrovent 0.5 mg and Albuterol 5.0 mg.
Transport completed smoothly with no complications reported.
The patient was unremarkable during transport and showed controlled symptoms.
Arrhythmia remained in AFib baseline as captured in the EKG transmission.
No additional complaints after initial assessment.
All transport checks were performed per facility and legal protocol.
Documentation is complete and flagged as medical.
Transfer process has been completed per procedure.
All activities pre-handoff have been documented and listed in the reports.
Continued monitoring equipment in place and all readings transmitted.
